description: PostgreSQL is a relational database service. In labs and CTFs, it may expose application data, users, password hashes, configuration values, and useful metadata after valid credentials are found.
why_prioritize: PostgreSQL can contain application users, credentials, tokens, settings, and sensitive lab data.
Database credentials are often reused or stored in web application configuration files.
Authenticated database access can reveal relationships between services, users, and application logic.

enumeration_steps.title: Check version and service information
enumeration_steps.reason: Version and basic service information help identify the PostgreSQL server and guide later authenticated checks.

enumeration_steps.title: Connect with discovered credentials
enumeration_steps.reason: PostgreSQL usually requires credentials, so use information found in configuration files, backups, or other services.

enumeration_steps.title: List databases and roles after login
enumeration_steps.reason: Database and role information helps you understand what the account can access.

enumeration_steps.title: Inspect useful tables carefully
enumeration_steps.reason: Tables may contain users, password hashes, tokens, settings, and application clues.
commands: SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public';

next_steps: Record the source of the credentials before testing them.
Try connecting to likely database names from web config files or application paths.
Check whether the same username appears in other services.
After login, list databases and roles before querying large tables.
List databases, roles, schemas, and tables.
Check the current user and privilege level.
Look for application-specific databases and user tables.
Keep queries small and focused so your output remains readable.
Identify username, email, password, hash, role, and active status columns.
Check whether password values are plaintext, hashed, or encoded.
Use recovered credentials carefully against related services in the lab.
Record table and column names for your report or notes.
Check what databases and schemas the role can access.
Look for admin or application owner roles.
Review permissions before attempting any advanced database action.
Use privileges as context for understanding the lab, not as a reason to run destructive commands.
Search for known issues affecting the exact version.
Confirm the version through Nmap and SELECT version() if authenticated.
Treat the version as a lead, not proof of vulnerability.
Continue credential and data-focused enumeration.

beginner_notes: PostgreSQL commonly needs a database name as well as a username and password.
Backslash commands like \l, \du, and \dt are psql client commands, not normal SQL.
Query small samples first using LIMIT so the output stays manageable.
Avoid destructive SQL commands unless the lab explicitly asks for them.
